input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its main hardware components include lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements, operating within the Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) domain for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functionality.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: (1) Data Monitoring, featuring a UI for real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks, along with data saving and report generation capabilities; (2) Computation Offloading, where the ACC system's lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation commands are offloaded from the vehicle's ECUs to cloud resources; and (3) Remote Management, enabling operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, and issue emergency braking commands, while developers utilize data collection for troubleshooting. The system is classified as a Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with Human-in-the-Loop per David and Bork [30].
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for hardware acceleration, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) middleware for inter-component communication, application modules implemented in Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink, and SQL database for structured data storage and reporting.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Synchronization accuracy of 100 ms is required for coordination between devices and computation offloading due to hardware limitations, whereas safety-critical vehicular standards mandate stricter synchronization (i.e., <100 ms).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture employs three distinct entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) each deployed on separate hardware components. The User Interface FE is split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents, while Data Storage lacks a natural FE alignment. Each FE is implemented as individual ROS2 packages containing ROS2 nodes, adhering to the ISO 23247 RA entity-based structure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system's orchestration follows ISO 23247 RA, organizing components into Physical Twin Entity (handling resource-specific and general automotive functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), Digital Twin Entity (data management and control), and User Entity (3D Visualization and Control Panel). Each entity is deployed on dedicated hardware, with FEs implemented as ROS2 packages. The Physical Twin Entity's design diverges from ISO 23247 by encompassing inherent system resources rather than only resource-specific functionality, affecting functionality ownership and engineering processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system employs Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking using DBSCAN and a Kalman filter for object tracking. Data is stored in an SQL database, and Data Translation components serialize data, align message types, and perform unit conversions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation components convert desired speed into motor voltage commands via a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller, with the lower level chassis responsible for controlling steering and speed.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure includes a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera for collecting data on detected objects in the vehicle's proximity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw point cloud data from LiDAR sensors, vehicle state parameters, motor actuation signals, and steering servo positions are collected by the Data Collecting FE via Wi-Fi UDP inter-entity communication. The Data Pre-Processing FE processes the point cloud data through clustering and tracking algorithms. All data streams undergo serialization, message type alignment, and unit conversion via the Data Translation component. Software component communication occurs over a ROS2 network.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for motor actuation and steering servo operations are generated by the ACC Controlling FE, serialized by the Data Translation component, and transmitted via ROS2 network to the Actuation FE. Specific details on command validation, authentication, feedback mechanisms, and execution confirmation are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components occurs over a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer protocol. Network topology, bandwidth requirements, latency constraints, reliability mechanisms, redundancy configurations, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247:2021 standard, Part 2 (Reference Architecture), defining an entity-based structure comprising Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities (FEs) implement specific tasks such as actuation, data transformation, and user interfacing, with implementation examples including OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for data collection and device control.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the Physical Twin as a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. It correctly identifies the main hardware components (lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements) and the operational domain (ADAS for ACC functionality). This is strongly supported by the sources: 'a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle by retrofitting it with autonomy components', and 'The physical architecture consists of three layers. The lower level chassis... The upper level chassis...'.

The extraction correctly details three services: Data Monitoring (UI for real-time visualization of vehicle state, projected path, object tracks, saving, and reports), Computation Offloading (lead vehicle detection, speed computation, and actuation commands offloaded to cloud), and Remote Management (enabling/disabling ACC, setting speed, emergency braking for operators; data collection for developers). It also correctly classifies the system as 'Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with Human-in-the-Loop' based on David and Bork [30]. This is well-supported by the text: 'Data Monitoring: ... allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports...', 'Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT...', 'Remote Management: ... enable/disable ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.', and 'falls under the Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop'.

The extraction precisely lists the specific tools, versions, and their roles as described in the source, including the SDK, middleware, programming languages, and database type. Supported by quotes: 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)... as the middleware to integrate application modules', 'implemented in Python 3.8 (rapid prototyping), C++ (ROS2 node lifecycle management), and MATLAB/Simulink', 'Data Storage... Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the specific synchronization and latency requirements defined in the requirements section. It correctly cites the 100 ms accuracy for clock synchronization and network latency, the rationale (hardware limitations and computation offloading), and the comparison to stricter vehicular standards.

The extraction accurately details the three entities, their deployment on separate hardware components, the splitting of the User Interface FE into 3D Visualization and Control Panel, and the specific implementation as ROS2 packages. It also correctly notes that Data Storage lacks a natural FE alignment. Quotes: 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'User Interface FE has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package'.

The extraction provides a comprehensive overview of the system's orchestration using ISO 23247 RA, correctly describing the roles of the Physical Twin Entity (including the inclusion of Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. It accurately identifies the deployment on dedicated hardware, the ROS2 implementation, and the specific deviation of the Physical Twin Entity regarding functionality ownership and engineering processes. Quotes: 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA', 'Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources... and can host general automotive functionality', 'impact the ownership of the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text focuses on the internal architecture (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entities) and the communication between them (e.g., ROS2, Wi-Fi). It does not describe integration with external systems, other Digital Twins, or enterprise systems beyond the generic mention of cloud compute offloading. Therefore, the assessment is correct.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

It correctly cites the Vedder Electronic Speed Controller, the conversion of speed to voltage, and the responsibilities of the lower level chassis. Quotes: 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'lower level chassis is responsible for controlling the steering and the speed'.

The extraction lists the exact sensing hardware specified in the document and correctly associates them with object detection in the vehicle's proximity. Quotes: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'detected objects in the vehicle’s proximity'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the source text lacks specific details regarding model fidelity requirements, validation methods, or uncertainty quantification as defined by the characteristic. While the text mentions 'high-fidelity computational reflections' as a general definition and references ISO 15622 for ACC functionality, it does not describe validation processes or error detection mechanisms.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
